text: The surgeon begins the supra-azygus and infra-azygus mobilization of the azygus vein, preparing to cut the vein as part of the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors in both the left and right hands to dissect the parietal pleura above and below the azygos vein.
The surgeon ligates and cuts the azygos vein as part of the initial step in performing a recurrent laryngeal lymph node dissection.
The assistant holds up the azygus vein, exposing the prevascular fascia, indicated by a right black arrow in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects within the prevascular fascia, with the aorta and the arch of the aorta visible behind the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the left recurrent laryngeal nerve by identifying the prevascular fascia, visible in the picture-in-picture (PIP) mode.
The thoracic duct is observed near the left border of the esophagus, with careful manipulation to prevent injury during the dissection.
The surgeon continues dissection along the prevascular fascia, fully exposing the aorta, including the ascending portion and the arch, while performing the dissection.

The thoracic duct is visible along the aorta and its adventitia, with the glistening white surface of the aorta fully exposed. The prevascular fascia is completely exposed up to the arch of the aorta, where the left recurrent laryngeal nerve hooks underneath.
The surgeon identifies and cuts the left vagus nerve in the infra-bronchial region, completing the transection of this nerve.
The surgeon identifies the visceral fascia, which is located between the esophagus and the trachea, preparing for further dissection in this area.
The surgeon uses cherry forceps, a cherry retractor, and a fan retractor to retract the trachea, performing a maneuver known as the tracheal dip or tracheal dive.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the left border of the trachea, maintaining a parallel trajectory to ensure precise separation of tissue layers.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the left lateral border of the trachea, following the visceral fascia. The dissection plane is marked by a white arrow in PIP mode, demonstrating the tracheal dive maneuver.

The surgeon dissects along the visceral pleura, shaded green in the PIB mode, adjacent to the left recurrent laryngeal lymph node packet. The visceral pleura is cut, revealing an avascular zone. The trachea is retracted towards the surgeon while the visceral fascia is incised.

The surgeon follows the visceral pleura to meet the visceral fascia and prevascular fascia behind the esophagus, lifting the left recurrent laryngeal lymph node packet along with the left recurrent laryngeal nerve and esophagus, adjacent to the aorta.
The surgeon performs dissection using a monopolar cautery in an avascular zone, with no additional energy source utilized.
The left recurrent laryngeal nerve is visible emerging from within the lymph node packet, highlighted by a right black arrow in the PIP mode.

The surgeon performs dissection above and below the left recurrent laryngeal nerve, carefully isolating the left recurrent laryngeal lymph node packet and associated lymphatic tissue to include them in the esophageal specimen.
The surgeon uses a fan retractor to pull the left main bronchus and trachea towards themselves, effectively exposing the area containing the left recurrent laryngeal lymph nodal packet.
The surgeon lifts the esophagus along with the lymph nodal tissue and lymph nodes, while the assistant retracts the trachea down and towards the surgeon on the right side to expose the surgical area.

The surgeon performs dissection within the lymph nodal packet, carefully pushing the recurrent laryngeal nerve down while avoiding the use of energy sources near the nerve. Bare scissors are used for dissection to prevent nerve damage.
The robotic right arm, equipped with monopolar scissors, carefully pushes down the recurrent laryngeal nerve while minimizing the use of energy sources in this sensitive region.
The tracheoesophageal groove is visibly cleared of fibro fatty and lymph nodal tissue, revealing the visceral fascia meeting the prevascular fascia.

The surgeon cuts the prevascular fascia, revealing the thoracic duct in the background. After ensuring the visceral fascia is completely cut, the dissection proceeds behind the esophagus towards the aorta and left pleura to excise the prevascular fascia, marked by a white arrow in the PIP mode.

The surgeon cuts the visceral fascia and prevascular fascia to expose the aorta located underneath and behind the esophagus. The fascia is dissected down to the infracoronal part, revealing the anatomical structures in this region.

The surgeon performs infracoronal dissection, exposing the right main bronchus and the subcoronal lymph nodal packet. The dissection is carried out close to the inferior borders of the right and left main bronchi.
The surgeon performs subcarinal dissection, exposing the entire carina and the left main bronchus. A small twig of tissue is cut in the membranous part of the bronchus or trachea.

The surgeon lifts the subcarinal lymph node from the pericardium, revealing the subcarinal area completely devoid of fibro fatty and lymphatic tissue. The surgical field appears relatively bloodless due to precise dissection along anatomical planes.
